content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movie Musicals that are accessible to people that don't like musicals? Any hidden gems?

**Score:** 46
**Body:** Covid-19 era got me into musicals. All the performances are so sincere and upbeat. It really is a special genre. I watched the two West Side Stories, Grease 1&2 and Rocky Horror Picture Show. I even warmed to Les Miserables (2012).

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Little Shop of Horrors! 


Also, I second Once- such a gorgeous film.
2. Blues Brothers.

The Muppet Movie.
3. Jesus Christ Superstar is my favorite movie all up. I'm not Christian either 
4. South Park: Bigger, Longer, and Uncut.
5. Dr. Horribles Sing A Long Blog
6. Hedwig and the Angry Inch
7. "Singing In The Rain". It's a classic and pretty funny as a bonus.
8. Cabaret.  The dialogue isn't sung.  Any singing is done in the context of the characters actually performing on a stage. The songs and performances are brilliant and subversive.   They carry a ton of weight, but they're fun.   I fucking love this movie.
9. *Once*, 2007 and *The Commitments*, 1991. 
10. Sound of Music is good and it's such a classic. Also Fiddler on the Roof and South Pacific. I grew up on musicals my grandma had like every Rogers and Hammerstein musical ever made. 

## 2. Give me romance movies that people would describe as horror

**Score:** 15
**Body:** I’m talking about movies that emotionally destroy you and are about romances so horrific and tragic that forever alter the way you see love.

Movies that I’ve watched that I think fit this example are:

La La Land

500 days of summer

Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

Like Crazy

Blue Valentine

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Passengers (2016)
2. Brokeback Mountain

My Girl

Atonement

One Day
3. [What Dreams May Come (1998)](https://www.themoviedb.org/movie/12159-what-dreams-may-come)
  - I saw that movie at the worst possible time in my life after losing my fiance to suicide and I will never ever see it again but it was beautifully made. 
4. To me, Once gives off similar vibes to La La Land
5.  Hm, interesting. I would describe none of these movies that way. Poignant and wistful, maybe.

For heartbreaking romance, *In the Mood for Love* is my go-to. It’s also the greatest film of the last 25 years.
6. Me Before You
7. City of Angels with Nicholas Cage and Meg Ryan. 
8. Gone Girl (2014)

The Lobster (2015)
9. Misery

## 3. Films like Wim Wenders' Perfect Days (2023)?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Hi, all—long-time lurker, first-time poster. Does anyone have recommendations for films like Wim Wenders' *Perfect Days* (2023)? 

For context, my partner recently watched this film for the first time and has commented something like "I wish I could watch another version of this" several times. He enjoys slice-of-life character studies and would like to see more films about similar subjects and themes (appreciating the everyday, building community through unexpected but meaningful relationships, etc.). 

I would love to present him with a list of other, similar films to check out. Thanks for reading!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You might like Hirokazu Kore-eda's movies. Many of them, like Still Walking 2008 or After Life 1998, have a calm, thoughtful slice of life vibes.

Janet Planet 2023 is very slice of life and meditative with the backdrop of rural Massachusetts.

Little Forrest 2018 is very gentle and slice of life focused on cooking and country life. 

You may also like Train Dreams 2025 , Spring Summer Fall Winter and Spring, 2003, Paterson 2016 and Columbus 2017.
  - I'm upvoting for Kore-Eda movies!  Our Little Sister also meets the criterion I think
  - Adding to the recommendation of Janet Planet. You also get an amazing performance by a 10 year old girl.
2. The Black Stallion has some action/adventure components, but it’s also beautiful and peaceful and has a similar vibe. 

Straight Story

Eat, Drink, Man, Woman offers the same calming peacefulness with cooking scenes, but does have more plot. 

Childhood (documentary)
3. Columbus (2017)

The Station Agent

4. Sweet Bean (2015)

Paterson (2016)
5. You might wanna check out Paterson 2016. It’s got that same chill, slice-of-life vibe about enjoying the small everyday stuff. Columbus 2017 is another good one too if you’re looking for somthing quiet and reflective like Perfect Days. Kinda the same slow, peacefull energy
6. Excellent recommendations all around in this thread.

Few slice of life American indies:

- Princess Cyd
- Myth of the American Sleepover

Both skew a bit younger, coming-of-age.

7. I‘ll recommend Whisper of the Heart (1995) and Taxi (2015)! 
8. Its more stressful cousin is Late Shift (2025). Mostly a nurse doing her job at a late shift in a hospital.

If you want the same tempo, check out Jeanne Dielman, 23, quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles (mich darker though).

## 4. What are your go-to plane movies for long flights?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** I have a 13-hour flight coming up soon and I’m looking for movie recommendations!

I’m open to pretty much anything as long as it’s entertaining and easy to watch on a plane. I especially love comedies, thrillers, and movies with good plot twists, but honestly anything that’s just really good and keeps you hooked works for me.

I’m not really in the mood for super complicated or heavy films. I’m looking for something lighter, engaging, and easy to follow that makes the time pass quickly.

Thank you so much!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Remember the Titans
2. It doesn’t really fit with the vibe you indicated, but in my last long flight I watched through all three LOTR movies, start to finish, long versions. Definitely entertaining, but also I’ve seen it enough that it was fine of I dozed off for 20 min here and there
3. Ghost dog: way of the samurai 
4. I watched gravity for the first time on a flight recently. Right as she was descending to earth our plane also entered its descent. Shit was immersive as hell.
5. Bond or Stephen king movies. Not just the good ones either. Living daylights, graveyard shift, Moon raker, thinner are all on the menu. Theres something comforting about pulpy genre pieces 
6. Lord of the Rings extended cuts. Got you covered for the flight back.
7. The first Knives Out, the Rush Hour trilogy, the Prestige! 
8. Interstellar 

Any(all) the Jason Bourne movies
9. Hangover trilogy
10. The only time I have enough free time for an Avatar movie is on a flight

## 5. “Normal” films with underlying, almost out of place supernatural elements and themes that catch you off guard

**Score:** 14
**Body:** My favorite kind of media / literature and I’m looking for more! Think Blood Meridian, The Sopranos, True Detective, Red Dead Redemption, etc

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. -	Cure (1997)
-	Fargo Season 2
-	No Country for Old Men (2007) scratched a little bit of the itch for a Blood Meridian adaptation, also from Cormac McCarthy
-	The Outsider (2020)
-	Twin Peaks
-	The Wailing (2016)
-	Border (2018)
-	Angel Heart (1987)
-	Apocalypse Now (1979)
-	The Green Mile (1999)
  - Forgive me, maybe it's this semi-drowsy zone I'm in aka dinner afterglow---but I'm not registering as for anything supernatural in the Coens' masterpiece (among others they've pieced with sheer mastery).  Help a guy out?
2. The Life of Brian


3. The Vast of Night (2019) 
4. Being There
5. Personal Shopper 
6. The Rapture

“This isn’t really happening is it??”

7. The Man From Earth 
8. **Magnolia**, 1999
9. Fanny and Alexander. Religion is a big theme in the movie, so not completely out of place, but the little slips of surrealism/supernatural are fairly surprising.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. 27 years ago today, ‘THE MATRIX’ was released in theaters.

**Score:** 1361

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Awesome movie.
  - Many of us were rolling our eyes when the first trailers came out. In the 90s so far you've had Hackers and The Net and this looked like it was just gonna be more of that same bullshit. 

Never been more happy to be wrong. :)
2. god damn it, I'm old. saw in in the theater on release, thought i could do anything for like a whole week. 
  - Coming out of the cinema after watching the Matrix is something I'll never forget.
    - I remember thinking: “They finally did it.”
3. Talk about stealing someone’s fire!  The MATRIX debuted less than two months before that little studio house film called THE PHANTOM MENACE.

![gif](giphy|WuVdPqDSsRbjy)
4. The opening scene was bonkers.  
  - Lieutenant: I think we can handle one little girl...  I sent two units, they're bringing her down now.

Agent Smith: No lieutenant, your men are already dead.
5. One of my best memories in life is a guy who saw this while high describing the plot of the movie to me (before I saw it) while we got high and walked home. I don’t think he got it, but boy did it sound amazing 
6. Still holds up, watch it once a year or so.

## 2. Happy 83rd birthday to Christopher Walken!! Do you have a favorite character he played?

**Score:** 1051
**Body:** I’ll start. Max Zorin in A View To A Kill, Captain Coon jn Pulp Fiction, Morty in Click and The Emperor in Dune Part Two 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. True Romance

![gif](giphy|lodxJmETc4bqPYv0uw)
2. ![gif](giphy|xT1XGKfc0gwXshqA80)
3. ![gif](giphy|qpADSJEkvdlK0)

Balls of Fury
4. https://i.redd.it/5n6td1mq2esg1.gif

The Prophecy
5. ![gif](giphy|l2SpLxM76MQXCclRS)
6. “I hid this uncomfortable chunk of metal up my ass for 2 years”
7. ![gif](giphy|yBnI9iojWdID6)
8. Deer Hunter
9. Christopher Walken, its the only role he has ever played. And to perfection
10. The King of New York 

## 3. The stellar Ewan McGregor turns 55 today. Happy birthday to him!

**Score:** 293

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|fnR1k3CZLQt3y)
  - Choose a fucking big television 
2. Why the photo gotta be black and white??? I thought it's not a birthday... 😖😭
  - I haven't thought about it when I was picking collage on Pinterest 😂 I thought this one is very retro vibe and I liked it, so I have chosen this one 
3. 8 more years and he’s as old as Alec Guinness was in the original Star Wars
4. His motorcycle journeys are my biggest point of jealousy towards Ewan. The movie star bit seems cool too though. 
5. Big Fish
6. I never learned how to spell his name... is it the same like "even" or the word (h)eaven without the h?
  - I spell it as /ˈjuːən/ (YOO-ən)  cause in Russian they wrote it as Юэн Маккрегор and I just checked it's the right way of pronouncing his name
    - I'm more confused than before 😅

## 4. Is it true a lot film bros and just guys in general used to hate on DiCaprio during his 90s Teen Idol-Titanic era?

**Score:** 243

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yeah kinda. Back then a lot of guys saw him as just a “pretty boy” from Titanic and didn’t take him seriously. It was more of a reaction to how huge he got with that audience.
  - Because they didn't watch What's Eating Gilbert Grape, which was 4 years before Titanic. If anyone watched that movie they would know he is a seriously talented actor. 
    - or This Boy's Life from '93 too.
      - or The Basketball Diaries
    - Former hater here. I did see Gilbert Grape when I was a teen and just thought his character was annoying.  It wasn't til I saw The Beach in 2000 that my whole thinking of Leo's ability as an actor changed.
  - Yeah it wasn’t until the Catch Me If You Can/Aviator/Departed/Blood Diamond run that he had mainstream movies where his acting was acclaimed over just his pretty-boy image. After Titanic, I was skeptical of his acting ability (hadn’t seen Gilbert Grape). The Beach (which is bonkers but fun Danny Boyle), Man in the Iron Mask (mediocre and forgettable), and even Gangs of NY didn’t really showcase his talent that much. He was fine in Gangs, but he had the misfortune of acting against an electric Daniel Day Lewis, and Cameron Diaz (who I think is a pretty good actress in other films), was miscast, so it seemed like Leo didn’t have a lot of chemistry. He was genuinely great in Catch Me If You Can.

As an aside, one of his best talents is that he probably has some of the best taste of a mainstream actor in my lifetime. He almost always works with great directors on interesting projects. They don’t always succeed, but they are always genuine efforts at great films. No cash grabs.
    - Basketball diaries, never gets mentioned and its one of his best  
2. Yeah. Used to get the same type of hate Bieber & Pattinson did. 
3. Yep, a lot of people just saw him as a pretty boy and overexposed like Chalamet until he started working with Scorsese, specifically in The Departed.
4. As a teen I remember I thought he was a giant douchebag, but that was pre-Titanic. I can't remember why, though. 

## 5. Robert De Niro and Joe Pesci are widely considered one of Hollywood's greatest on-screen duos. Working together in such classics like, Raging Bull, Goodfellas, Casino and The Irishman, they’re definitely legends in Hollywood. Who else are some some of your favorite on-screen acting duos?

**Score:** 223

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jack Lemon and Walter Matthau 
2. Simon Pegg and Nick Frost, my absolute favourites.

![gif](giphy|26BRHUXXwjHcNeIRq)
3. Newman & Redford

6. ![gif](giphy|ASmQEEypVq9Ik)
7. Steve Martin and Martin Short. 
8. ![gif](giphy|xYTExvnaF4KW1eaYZY|downsized)
9. Clooney and pitt

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. In Poor Things (2023), was the message really feminist sexual liberation?

**Score:** 100
**Body:** So I just finished Poor Things. I think it is a very interesting movie and gave it a 4.5.

Through passive exposure and looking at online discussions, I know *Poor Things* is very controversial among feminists. They say it is an overly sexualized story written by a man that shows the development of a young woman through the male gaze.

Now I have thoughts. I agree that the use of sex in this story was excessive. The core idea of a child’s mind in a grown body exploring the vast, cruel world could have been done without sex being such an important part. I think it is a great missed opportunity to not further explore things through the philosophical lens that was teased during the boat section.

That said, I don't think it was wholly inappropriate. The particular circumstances and time period (Victorian era) the character was placed in could explain why this would be such an important part.

Bella was a naive, beautiful girl, so her being exploited is not impossible. Also, a child’s mind seeks pleasure in any way, with no regard for social or moral appropriateness. For a normal child, they would not have these sexual drives yet, but Bella is in a fully grown body. All of this makes sense to me—that in her early stages, she would seek bodily pleasure without a care, as shown in the movie.

As she developed further, we can see how she started to understand the world and the people around her more. She found sleeping with Mark Ruffalo's character distasteful due to her dislike of his personality. She showed growth, moving away from blindly seeking pleasure.

In Paris, she took up a job in a brothel. Now you can say that sex work is inherently bad, but that doesn't really go against the movie. It wasn't saying sex work was a good job that women should do. It was clearly shown as a means to an end. It pays and fulfills her initial pleasure needs. As she went on in the brothel, though, she started finding most of her sexual interactions to be displeasurable, pushing her away from the line of work and furthering her understanding of herself, understanding her own preferences better. This whole section really rapidly led to her full development as a fully grown person.

So after my very rough initial understanding of the movie, at no point in the story did Bella's choice of seeking sex feel empowering. Most often, it was either her poor understanding of herself or necessity that led to the act. She managed to learn and develop from it thanks to her eagerness and curiosity, not her lustfulness or desire for freedom.

My biggest takeaway from this movie was the journey of development and growth Bella went through, going from the childishness in the beginning to a fully grown woman by the end. Her gender definitely played a big part in how things turned out, but to reduce the entire message to “she just wanted sex” and frame that as the film promoting sexual liberation feels like a misreading of what the story is actually doing.

The majority of sex in the movie was shown in a negative light, probably except for the one time Bella's prostitute friend performed oral sex on her. The exploitative, powerful white males were all clearly villains and were all punished, so I don't get how this movie is so problematically male gaze-y and inappropriate.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the feminist commentary comes from dropping a female sexual agent with no internalized conception of the patriarchy into the world and having her respond with violent animosity to things other women more beaten down by culture would just take. The empowerment is seeing someone not wearing the same yoke. She isn’t “liberated” because her behavior isn’t in reaction to anything, she just is existing as she naturally finds herself and her sexuality is a part of that. But of course even she gets dominated and forced into a marriage with a cruel piece of shit. I think the film overall was more focused on the evils of the restrictions of our entrenched society, which means there are elements of female sexual liberation but the overall tone ends up darker and more compromised, more about the fact of oppression than an exploration of liberation.
  - This is spot on. My own summary (which is not at odds with yours) would be: this is a movie that is critical of patriarchy, without pretending it *wasn't* made by a man (and thus isn't remotely immune from the male gaze). I think Garland's movies Ex Machina & Men are in the same category: more anti-man than pro-woman. If a feminist male filmmaker wants to engage with sexual politics, I think this is a fine way to do it. (Way way way better than whatever the fuck Zack Snyder thinks he was doing in Sucker Punch.)
2. As someone who loved the book, the movie really bothered me. There's a lot more nuance in the book that's lost on screen. For instance, near the end, it's implied that >!the whole story is the sex fantasy of her husband, who is jealous of her accomplishments and trying to discredit her!<. 


The novel is told primarily from his perspective, and only at the end, do we get a glimpse into Bella's own mind.
  - I loved the film, and still do for its many merits, but after reading the book I was rather devastated that it is a pretty shallow adaptation in many ways. I think McNamara and Lanthimos saw it more as this transgressive and modernist fantasia update to Frankenstein and Mary Shelley and just stayed in that lane. 
  - Loved the book
>!There's also the part where she becomes an  active pacifist with an agenda to make the world a better place. A notion that is immediately counterd by the macabre fact that her last letter is sent right at the eve of the first world war.!<


The movie was quite disappointing imo. Letting go of the whole unique structure of the book and its inherent dark playfulness. Instead it was mainly a vehicle for Emma Stone to do some fun acting. Which is nice I guess.
  - This is my feeling too. It's why I find the whole 'it was clearly written and directed by a man' criticism, or rather the pushback against it, quite amusing. Putting aside the ending, the small deviations from the book felt unnecessary. For example, Bella's time in the brothel served as motivation for her to become a doctor in the novel. It's not something the book lingers on. The way the brothel chapter is framed between film and novel is night and day.

I'm very 50/50 on Lanthimos. I believe he has a pre-occupation with being provocative and that's to the detriment of films like Poor Things. It's not a watered down adaption, it's a deliberate misreading.
3. Here is my perspective on the film. It questions the “social reality” of all of society and the choices we have made on what is proper and appropriate and right. 

It questions thought and ideas and the meaning of words and language. Our social reality is all the rules and systems we have placed on top of the actual physical reality of the world. 

I think every one of Yorgos films is exploring this same idea. 

So you have an adult body and “woman” with a child’s brain in it. So from our visual sense the men see a woman of age and want to have sex with her. But with a child’s brain she is effectively at the beginning what we would call intellectually disabled. But they also don’t all realize she has a child’s brain. She just seems “fun” and “free” and “quirky” to them. 

As mark ruffalo looks to take her out into the world, he tells her on the roof that he is a bon vivant. That he cares not for the social mores of the world, and he does his own thing. 

But as we go we find he cares very much for social mores. This is shown acutely at the table scene where he tried to get her to only say three or four things. 

Same goes for the sex, or better yet, the furious jumping. For her is literally just fun and pleasurable. She didn’t get caught in her feels. She doesn’t know that she’s supposed to grow attached and that it now “means she is his”. 

But we also see him again for who he truly is. He is not what he believes himself to be. He grows attached. He wants ownership of her. She drives him mad because she is everything he wants to believe himself to be, but is not. 

She is the true vigilante of social mores. She has him bested in every facet. Because she never learned the “rules” we made up. She fits the visual of an adult, with none of our baggage. 

Yorgos is shinning a light on how ridiculous we are. How absurd all our definitions and rules really are. 
Things we think are normal in real life are shown to be pretty ridiculous in his films. 

Same thing in dogtooth. Same in the lobster.

They escape the hotel in the lobster merely to bind themselves again to rules in the forest. 

Dogtooth taking children and making words mean other things. Depriving them of experience so they become what we call weird. 

I think Poor Things is just his underlying theme pushed to an extreme degree. 

Why do we do what we do? Does it make any sense? And what feels so normal to us IRL tends to look pretty absurd when it gets framed just right in a Yorgos movie.
4. It’s not a movie about agency if the way the movie is told is exploitive. Then it’s an exploitive movie disguised as a movie about agency. If it’s “genius” then I don’t need another genius to tell stories about women. It’s so exhausting. 

Vladimir on Netflix does a better job at examining roles. Gone girl. It’s like. Figure out that we don’t need another movie about sex workers who game the system. 
To then call it literature because she is learning. 

I’ve never hated a movie so much. 

5. The problem with satire is that you have to indulge in the acts that you are critiquing, and it’s easy to misunderstand critique as support. 

Satire is accelerationist in that it pushes things further to try to offend people because the core issue is offensive - yet still acceptable. 

If the problem is we burn women at the stake as witches - a satirist would pour gasoline on the pyre while twirling their mustache. 

Does the act make you think “wow, enjoying it that much is cruel”, or “hey, why are we burning these women?”

My interpretation wasn’t that it was trying to scandalize or make an outside moral judgement of her actions. She was dedicatedly rational in her choices and the society portrayed in the film didn’t like it. I don’t know how someone can say it was overly sexual without feeding into the moralization of sexuality of women. 

Is yorgos a sex fiend? Idk. Sexual liberation seems like a component of fully self actualized liberation so idk how we avoid it considering how fundamental it is to human existence.
6. Love Poor Things personally and agree with your points. In regard to female sexual liberation, I saw it more as interogating that idea and acknowledging that female sexual liberation can be apropriated by men (Ruffalo's character) and capitalism (the brothel, not that sex work is categorically bad in vacuum, but under those conditions it certainly is exploitative). 

## 2. What if asking “what does this film mean?” is not enough?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I’ve been thinking about how often film discussion collapses into the question of meaning too early.

What does it mean? What is it saying? What is the message?

Those are fair questions, but I’m starting to think they can also flatten the experience if they are asked too soon. They assume that the main job of a film is to arrive at an interpretable statement, when a lot of the strongest films seem to work by moving the viewer through a sequence before meaning ever fully settles.

Some films first pull you into a mood or sensory state. Others make you live inside a character’s tension before you understand the larger pattern. Others orient you through story, then destabilize that story, then leave you trying to reassemble what actually happened. And some seem to withhold stable meaning almost entirely, but still leave behind a very strong internal impression because of the order they moved through feeling, perception, story, and reflection.

That makes me think “what does this mean?” may be too narrow by itself. A better question might be: what kind of movement did this film take me through? Did it start with feeling and only later become legible? Did it begin with narrative clarity and then widen into ambiguity? Did it move from observation into dread, from dread into interpretation, from interpretation into some larger sense of meaning? Or did it deliberately refuse one of those steps?

The more I pay attention, the more it seems like part of what separates forgettable films from haunting ones is not just theme, symbolism, or plot quality, but the sequence in which the film asks the viewer to process what they’re seeing.

I’m posting this because I’m trying to get better at talking about films in a way that doesn’t reduce them too quickly to “what they mean.” I’m more interested lately in the internal order of the experience and whether some films stay alive in memory precisely because they moved in the right sequence rather than because they yielded a neat interpretation.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Agreed. 

Part of the problem is approaching movies — or any artwork — as a problem to be solved, like a Rubik's cube that will produce the correct "answer" if you just twist and turn it enough. 

Another part is assuming that there is only one possible meaning. Again, most artworks can't be boiled down to one "meaning," in part because they aren't created to be a complicated way of delivering a simple message. 

That doesn't mean artists might not have some thoughts about things they're trying to say, but approaching any artwork is not only an exercise in decoding what the artist intended to say. Their opinion about their own work is not a definitive answer about meaning or lack of meaning. 

Any approach to movies — or any artwork — benefits from treating it as an experience, maybe an exploration. It's worth considering the ways movies might support (or refute) specific interpretations, taking what is actually there on screen as evidence. But most art, particularly good art, supports multiple possible approaches, even possibly interpretations that conflict, without meaning any of them is right or wrong. 

"What does it mean?" is not a terrible question, and can be a guide, but I think you should be suspicious of anyone claiming that this question can be definitively and comprehensively answered for any artwork. Meaning relies on the movie, but it's not only something hidden within the movie.
  - > A story is a way to say something that can't be said any other way, and it takes every word in the story to say what the meaning is. You tell a story because a statement would be inadequate. When anybody asks what a story is about, the only proper thing is to tell them to read the story. The meaning of fiction is not abstract 
meaning but experienced meaning.

–Flannery O'Connor
2. I'm 100% with you. The way in which people analyze things is often a pet peeve of mine. When I was first getting into film as a teenager I wasn't thinking about what films "mean" at all, I was drawn in by the artistic beauty, cinematography, music, emotion.

Sometimes I find that a macro-technical analysis can amplify how powerful a film comes across to a viewer if it helps them feel the emotions that the filmmakers were going for through how they presented what they presented, but all too often that kind of analysis has a flattening effect, as you said.
3. I agree. A reviewer, if they want to cap off a review which is full of praise for a film may feel some responsibility to say what the take-away is, what all the praiseworthy aspects they’ve delineated come together to communicate in terms of a vision or philosophy of the writer and director. In sum, the film represents X message, or presents a character whose story is newly iconic of X theme or truism, or the film’s conflict represents X debate, etc.

It’s true that there’s such a thing as subtext or allegory, and so on. But the desire to get right into this kind of explanatory mode of discussing the object that is the film should sometimes be resisted for a bit, and summary and appreciation of the elements and sequence of narrative events shouldn’t be discounted as being a very worthwhile part of the process of criticism / interpretation. Some films might seem to be clearly about some theme or issue. With others, consideration of such connections or implications, will be a more subtle read, as they’ll at first appear to be primarily just “about” their own narrative events.

4. Maybe a distinction that's only slight, but to me makes sense: I tend to approach films through the lens of "what is this about?" rather than "what does this film mean?" Because I think taking the approach of "what does it mean?" assumes that a film has one interpretation, like you note, that is the "correct take." That it is explicitly trying to say a specific thing. 

But sometimes a movie is \*about\* something without \*meaning\* something. Like the film is about grief or how people process it, but it's not explicitly saying anything about the ways in which people do or should process grief. Maybe that seems a minor difference, but to me they're kinda asking different questions. Or also like, "What is this about?" could also be a film that's like, "It's just a movie about vibes that make you uneasy" or something. Ya know?

And also when examining "what is it about?" is when my friends and I tend to talk about things like lighting and cinematography and score and things, because we're talking about how something was conveyed. 
5. I don't get why these would be mutually exclusive in any way? Of course a movie is more memorable if it's more enticing to the senses and of course a movie is deeper if it includes symbolism. 

How do these ideas contrast?
6. We've got generations of people raised and taught to engage the world on a "question -> answer" or "problem -> solution" basis. That's how we're educated in school, that's how we're trained on the job. The typical pattern is to pressure us to arrive at a correct conclusion, not sit and ruminate.

I've long felt that a huge swath of the public sees movies or shows or books as a challenge, like it's possibly trying to trick them or trying to slip in some surprise or gotcha. It's like people can get nervous, almost paranoid, if they don't have some definite, absolute conclusion to something they experience. It's the sort of habit that prompts people to rush their thought process, to jump to conclusions, and so their ways of exploring media are super-direct and hasty and superficial.

They're probably more concerned with knowing what to say about a movie/show/etc. than exploring on their own how they feel, or something like that. After all, there is a community element to engaging with fiction, similar to politics or sports. It's a way to socialize, a thing to socialize about, have a shared experience with it. And for a lot of people that social aspect is more important than the artistic, I suppose.
7. >That makes me think “what does this mean?” may be too narrow by itself. A better question might be: what kind of movement did this film take me through? Did it start with feeling and only later become legible? Did it begin with narrative clarity and then widen into ambiguity? Did it move from observation into dread, from dread into interpretation, from interpretation into some larger sense of meaning? Or did it deliberately refuse one of those steps?

Your 5 'better' questions are basically renditions of 'what does this mean?,' as they're asking for the inner cognitive process of this or that audience. To put it bluntly, by asking 'what does this mean?' the viewer is engaging with the hermeneutic dimension of the film. He or she not being verbally nor academically equipped to articulate different versions of the very same inquiry doesn't exclude the core sense of their concern.
8. I agree, and the flip-side of that is that meaning doesn't answer for other shortcomings.

I can't tell you how often I see criticism (not just in film, but in literature) dismissed by fans with "this misses the whole point of the story." As though the intention, the meaning, justifies every decision made in depicting that meaning, or that the message itself is sacrosanct simply because the director or author meant it to be that way. A great example a few years back was *Don't Look Up*, which was panned by many critics, and there was this bizarre online movement to insist that the things they were complaining about were intentionally frustrating elements to reflect actual frustration, and a disingenuous push to insist that some of those reviewers only disliked it because of political leanings. Couldn't possibly be that people who review movies for a living have an expectation that those movies aim a little higher than simply agreeing with their worldview. 

A director can misstep. They can have a point that missteps so spectacularly as to put their foot in their mouth. They can also have a fine idea that doesn't work on the screen. They can have a brilliant methodology that only connects with a small portion of viewers, and other viewers aren't *wrong* in their criticisms just because they're not part of that audience.

I think too often criticism (especially in the current internet age) gets boiled down to trying to arrive at consensus, and a reviewer who doesn't fit the consensus "got it wrong," and a big part of that revolves around importance placed on meaning. Art is not only what you say but how you say it, and acknowledging the former doesn't mean we have to cut the latter slack. It also allows for a broad spectrum of opinions and criticism, criticism that disagrees with other criticism, and criticism that readers don't necessarily agree with either. We should be celebrating a variety of views, not chastising them for having the nerve to step away from the pack.
9. Yeah I think this nicely captures how some films can take you through what mostly feels like "dream logic" and be impactful and powerful even if there may not be any clear "message" at all. Getting too stuck on literal meaning can make you miss the magic of *vibes*

It also can irritate me if I enjoyed a film for its atmosphere and then I see people picking it apart for "plot holes" or other more literal things like that.

## 3. What Stuck With Me About Dead Poets Society

**Score:** 17
**Body:** I just finished watching Dead Poets Society yesterday, and honestly, I can’t find anything bad to say about it. I’m not a film critic or anything, but it’s one of those movies that just… stays with you.

What stood out to me most was how every character leaves a mark. Each of them has their own story, their own presence, and as the film goes on, you feel all of it layering on top of you.

My favorite character was Todd Anderson.

I think it’s because I relate to him—the anxious kid, awkward and unsure, suddenly thrown into a world that expects him to speak when he’s spent his whole life staying quiet.

My favorite scene was when Mr. Keating pulls him in front of the class and forces him—not cruelly, but insistently—to face what’s inside him instead of dismissing it as nothing. When the class starts laughing and Todd shuts his eyes, and Keating says, “Forget them. FORGET them.”

If that can land with you, it can change your world. Its the most important part of the scene.

Few scenes in cinema hold a significance to me more than this scene.

And when Todd finallly lets the words out. They hit like a bag of bricks

I keep replaying the image in my head:

A sweaty-toothed madman with a stare that pounds against my brain.

His hands reach out and choke me, and all the while he’s mumbling—

“Truth, truth.”

Like a blanket that always leaves your feet cold.

You pull at it, stretch it, but it’s never enough.

You kick it, fight it—it still won’t cover any of us.

From the moment we enter crying,

to the moment we leave dying,

it only ever covers your face

as you wail and scream.

And then there was Charlie Dalton.

At first, he just comes off as the loud, rebellious one—the guy who doesn’t take anything seriously. But the more I watched, the more he felt like the opposite of Todd in a strange way.

Where Todd is silent because he’s afraid to speak, Dalton is loud because he refuses to be silenced.

He leans fully into what Mr. Keating teaches—maybe even more than anyone else. The whole “Nuwanda” thing, the newspaper article, bringing girls into Welton—it’s reckless, yeah, but it also feels like he’s the only one willing to actually challenge the system instead of just surviving it.

But at the same time, there’s something almost sad about it.

Because you can tell that underneath all that confidence, it’s still a performance in its own way. Like he has to be loud, has to push boundaries, because stopping would mean facing the same pressures everyone else is quietly suffocating under.

And unlike Todd, who grows inward and finds his voice, Dalton burns outward—and gets punished for it.

And of course, Mr. Keating.

I don’t think I’ve ever loved a teacher in a film more. Robin Williams plays him in a way that feels so alive—like he isn’t just teaching poetry, he’s trying to wake something up in them—the exercise about conformity.

The boys start walking in the courtyard, and slowly, without realizing it, they fall into the same rhythm. Same pace. Same sound. It’s almost uncomfortable to watch, how quickly individuality disappears.

And then Keating points it out—that quiet, creeping danger of becoming like everyone else without even noticing.

That’s why Dalton stands out so much in that moment.

When Keating asks who would not be joining them, Dalton says, “Exercising the right not to walk.”

It’s such a simple line, but it carries so much weight.

Because that’s the whole idea of Keating’s teaching—not just to be different for the sake of it, but to choose for yourself. To be aware. To resist that automatic pull toward conformity.

And then there’s Neil Perry.

I don’t even know how to talk about him without it hurting a little.

If Todd is fear, and Dalton is defiance, then Neil feels like something in between—someone who knows what he wants, but doesn’t have the freedom to reach it.

That scene where he says, “I am trapped,” is probably one of the most honest moments in the film.

Because he isn’t confused. He isn’t unsure. He knows exactly who he is and what he loves. Acting isn’t a phase for him—it’s the one place where he feels alive. And that’s what makes it worse.

It’s not about finding himself.

It’s about being denied the right to be himself.

And everything around him keeps closing in—his father, the expectations, the structure of Welton itself. You can almost feel the walls tightening the closer he gets to that one moment of freedom on stage.

Because while Keating teaches them to seize the day, Neil is the one who tries—and pays the price for it.

And after everything, it doesn’t feel like a single tragedy. It feels inevitable, like the system was always going to win.

If you're learning about romanticism, look no further than this movie.

Yap away if you want about your thoughts on this movie

2. I'm just gonna leave this here, which is an extract (starting pg. 47) from Todd McGowan's *The End of Dissatisfaction  Jacques Lacan and the Emerging Society of Enjoyment*

A small comment. Todd's term for the permissive father figure, "the anal father", is Freudian, and thus controversial. Even more controversial is the current expression of the anal father, Donald Trump, who compels us to enjoy.

>OUR FRIEND,THE NEW AUTHORITY

>The contrast between the two fathers makes itself felt in Peter Weir’s Dead Poets Society (1989). Though the film clearly sides with the newly emergent anal father of enjoyment, it depicts his emergence through a struggle with traditional paternal authority. The fact that Weir sets the film in the 1950s and at an exclusive all-boys boarding school highlights the conflict between the two fathers and has the added effect of placing the anal father in the position
of the outsider. The filmic milieu offers the traditional father home-court advantage: this is a world in which traditional symbolic authority polices all enjoyment and demands its complete renunciation; symbolic authority’s overt
presence here serves to make subjects quite aware of their castration. This authority manifests itself conspicuously in the mise-en-scène of the film’s opening. The film begins with a new school year at the prestigious Wellton Academy. We see the students dressed formally and speaking to both teachers and parents in a highly respectful manner. When, at the opening assembly, the headmaster asks the students to identify the school’s “four pillars,” the students all rise and recite them in unison. From these initial shots of the film, it quickly becomes apparent that symbolic authority rules Wellton efficiently and thoroughly.

>Against this backdrop, the anal father emerges in the form of Mr. Keating (Robin Williams), a new English teacher. Unlike the other teachers and
administrators at Wellton—and unlike the parents of the boys he teaches—Keating does not preach obedience but encourages his students to find their own path to enjoyment, to, as he puts it, “seize the day.” Reciting Thoreau and Whitman, Keating tells them that they must avoid conformity to demands of symbolic authority and instead enjoy themselves. Keating’s first class illustrates the difference between his authority and the headmaster’s authority depicted in the opening sequence. Whereas the headmaster ordered and expected disciplined behavior from the students, Keating shows them pictures of past students from the school who are now dead, and he encourages them to enjoy themselves before they too are “food for worms.”
The struggle that ensues for the hearts of the students is clearly no struggle at all; traditional symbolic authority has no chance against Keating’s call for enjoyment. In response to Keating’s command that they pursue enjoyment,
some of the students come together to form the film’s titular group, a group that meets in a cave in the woods late at night, in defiance of school rules. This defiance, however, does not at first arouse the suspicion of the school authorities: because it takes place literally underground and under cover of darkness, none of the school authorities even notice it. Historically, traditional symbolic authority has always permitted such transgressions as long as the enjoyment
in them remained hidden and underground. The conflict develops only at the point when the boys in the film bring their pursuit of enjoyment out into the open. When they unabashedly pursue enjoyment publicly, the figures of authority in the film respond with the full weight of the symbolic prohibition, demanding unequivocally that the enjoyment cease. This becomes most
apparent in the case of Neil Perry (Robert Sean Leonard), the leader of the Dead Poets Society.
Encouraged by Keating’s proclamations about the importance of finding one’s own individual enjoyment, Neil discovers that acting is his particular path, the way in which he enjoys. It is precisely this enjoyment, however, that Neil’s father prohibits, laying down the commandment that Neil give up this “acting nonsense.” As Alenka Zupancic concisely puts it, “Neil’s situation can be described as follows: he has his Thing—acting—but Father forbids it.” Neil’s father not only forbids this enjoyment, but he also demands that Neil take up a prescribed symbolic role: he tells Neil in no uncertain terms, “you’re going to go to Harvard, and you’re going to be a doctor.” Here, Neil’s father occupies the position of symbolic authority, and the commandment of this
authority is unambiguous: sacrifice your enjoyment for the sake of symbolic recognition and identity. Neil feels the crushing power of this symbolic authority over him and the dissatisfaction that its prohibition occasions. (In
fact, the prohibition of enjoyment even drives Neil to kill himself.) Faced with the clear functioning of symbolic authority, Neil has no respite from the experience of his castration—being barred from enjoyment—and its attendant dissatisfaction. These are the effects of the symbolic order that he—and other subjects in times of conspicuous symbolic authority—can’t help but feel.

>But Mr. Perry does not have the last word in Dead Poets Society. Even though Mr. Perry and the school authorities blame Mr. Keating for Neil’s suicide and even though they recognize the disruptive impact of Keating’s espousal of enjoyment, they are unable to extirpate his influence. At the end of the film, Wellton fires Keating for his role in the formation of the Dead
Poets Society and in Neil’s death, but the film’s famous final scene makes clear that Keating has won the hearts of the students. In that scene, the school’s headmaster takes over the English class formerly taught by Keating, and he attempts to return the class to the rigid, authoritative structure that predominates at Wellton. But as he is leaving the campus, Keating interrupts the class to retrieve his personal belongings. After gathering his things together, Keating opens the door to leave, and one of the students, Todd Anderson (Ethan Hawke), stands on his desk (a gesture Keating had earlier championed) and
proclaims, “Oh Captain, my Captain” (Whitman’s address to Lincoln, which Keating encouraged the students to apply to him). Many of the other students follow Anderson in his act, and they remain standing on their desks despite the headmaster’s repeated demands that they step down. The headmaster employs all of his symbolic authority on this occasion—even threatening expulsion—and it is still no match for Keating’s appeal. In this way, the film lays out the struggle between two competing modes of authority, and it leaves no doubt about the winning side. One of the reasons the anal father has prevailed over the traditional symbolic father is this personal appeal. Unlike the imposing symbolic father, the anal father, because he licenses our enjoyment rather than prohibiting it, seems much more approachable and kind.
Not only does the film depict the victory of the anal father over traditional symbolic authority, but it also reveals its investment in the anal father through the very way that it lays out the struggle. While the figures of traditional symbolic authority are clearly staking out an ideological position (championing the prohibition of enjoyment), Keating seems to be simply an innocent victim, guilty only of stimulating the boys to do what they wanted to do anyway. When
the headmaster blames Keating for Neil’s death, we cannot but feel the injustice of this: obviously it is Mr. Perry’s prohibition of Neil’s enjoyment—not Keating’s encouragement of it—that resulted in Neil’s suicide, since the suicide occurs immediately after Mr. Perry punishes Neil for his disobedience in pursuit of his love for acting. Thus, Keating appears as an innocent victim, a scapegoat for Mr. Perry’s own guilt in the death of his son. And this is how the film reveals the depth of its attachment to the new brand of authority that Keating represents: Dead Poets Society is invested in Keating (as a representative anal
father of enjoyment) to such an extent that it does not even depict his authority as authority. The film presents Keating as a benevolent teacher interested only in the welfare of his students, not as a rival authority to Mr. Perry or the headmaster. Precisely because he doesn’t appear in the guise of an alternate authority, Keating’s authority is all the more powerful—over both his students and over us as viewers of the film. It is nearly impossible to view the film without seeing Keating as the innocent victim of the cruelty of traditional symbolic authority. This situation alone indicates the degree of the film’s ideological
commitment to Keating and what he represents.
As Dead Poets Society demonstrates, the anal father himself always seems less than authoritative. Unlike traditional symbolic authority, the anal father appears in the guise of one of us; he’s on our side, not on the side of authority. But as one of us, he exerts his authority in ways that traditional symbolic
authority could never imagine. We aren’t suspicious of an authority who doesn’t appear to be an authority. Hence, Mr. Perry and the headmaster can only look on in envy at the authority Keating wields. When they stand on their desks in the film’s final scene, the students express their willingness to bow down to the new authority and eschew the old, thereby clearly demonstrating the power of the new.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There's many different kinds of avant-garde but if you want the ones that inspired Lynch then here's a five film starter pack

\- *Last Year at Marienbad.* I watched *Mulholland Drive* just yesterday and it feels connected directly to *Marienbad* somehow.  
\- *Meshes of the Afternoon*. Nice eerie short that basically inspired the dream logic of Lynch's work.  
\- *The Holy Mountain*. More psychedelic than a Lynch but gives you a deconstructed dream vision.  
\- *Scorpio Rising*. You can trace some of Lynch's fetishisms to Anger's short work.  
\- *As I Was Moving Ahead Occasionally I Saw Brief Glimpses of Beauty*. The least Lynchian of the 5, but a good entry way into the worlds of experimental documentary and personal filmmaking.
2. Firstly, I would heed what GUBEvision mentioned since you namechecked Lynch- the work of Maya Deren. Her most famous work is Meshes in the Afternoon. After that, watch her other work. 

Dimitri KIrsanoff's Menilmontant (1926) is another important film and highly recommended. 

The work of Kenneth Anger- I second GUBEvisions's rec of Scorpio Rising as well as his other work. 

The work of Chris Marker- films like La Jetee and Sans Soleil are absolutely essential.

Stan Brakhage's Dog Star Man is probably his best known work and best place to start.

  
I recommend the site [ubuweb.com](http://ubuweb.com) for i[ts large repository of experimental film ](https://www.ubuweb.com/index.html)as well as other forms of art.
3. I just watched yesterday Branded to Kill (1967) from Seijun Suzuki. It's a really cool surrealist, experimentalish post-modern take on the noir and yakuza genre. I highly recommend it. 
4. You might be interested in Tarkovsky’s stuff. His most popular movie is Stalker, but his most experimental film I’ve seen is Mirror. Long, meditative, dream-like sequences are his calling card, so much so that the word tarkovskian is used to describe similarly directed movies.

## 5. Is it possible for a film to be great if its story isn't very good but its visuals are?

**Score:** 3
**Body:** I've noticed that a lot of the time, people praise films mostly for their visual aspects, like the cinematography, framing, color, and overall look. But when I think about them later, the story or theme doesn't seem as memorable. If a film's emotional or story impact doesn't stick with me, I find it hard to call it truly great, even if the visuals are stunning at the time. A film can look great but still feel empty if there's nothing real to connect with underneath. Cinema is a visual medium by nature, and some filmmakers choose to focus on mood, atmosphere, and visual language over traditional narrative structure. They still make films that people enjoy. In those situations, the pictures themselves tell the story. This makes for an interesting conflict between style and substance. It makes me wonder if we put too much weight on how a film looks when we rate it, or if just how it looks is enough to call it great. 

I'm interested in how other people find this balance. Do you think a film can be meaningful just because it looks good, or does it need a strong story to go with it?

1. The color of pomegranates is a strong example of what you’re saying, just a sort of visual artifact of Armenian nationalism and identity.

5. A look at it differently: A movie doesn‘t always have to be meaningful in order to provoke a feeling in me. But in my book minor masterpieces exist where the visuals hit a certain spot for me.
6. If a painting can be “meaningful”, then of course a movie could be “meaningful” even without anything going on beyond the visuals. 

But I don’t think anyone is going to bring an example to this discussion that conclusively demonstrates that a great film can be a failure (or a non-starter) in *any* aspect beyond the visuals. And that’s fine, because movies are made up of many elements, and the best movies are firing on all cylinders.  

The strongest case I can make is for Matthew Barney, whose movies primarily function as filmed sculpture & performance art (with a soundtrack). At their best, his films approach the pinnacle of art (for my tastes), but the “narrative” aspect is so vague that I’d be quite lost without having read descriptions of what’s going on. Even this example – the best one I can think of – is going to fail because the vast majority of viewers will confidently state that these movies are terrible. And that’s fine, of course. It just tells us that cinema is multifaceted and we’re often left wanting more when one aspect wasn’t fully utilized.
7. I think that you can have a thin plot elevated by a stellar directing because form can be used as substance to nourish a plot. 

Meanwhile the greatest plot can be voided by bad directing, all your great substance can be lost if its either bland or amateurish, overstylised or not adequate.
8. I think the first Suspiria is a great example of this; the film is stunning but the plot and narrative are juvenile and a total afterthought. I think the remake helped a lot with this and it stands on its own two feet but it's so hard to not be enraptured by the original's visuals.
9. It’s not easy to even get a consensus on what is meant by good visuals. There was a post here a while ago about the cinematography of Hamnet, which the OP thought was poor. Many commenters agreed that it was bland or some variation of that thought. One person brought up the example of The Zone of Interest to make the point that their issue with Hamnet’s cinematography isn’t just that it’s digital, as TZOI is a digitally shot film that they think looks good. (These two films just happen to have both been shot by cinematographer Lukasz Zal.)

I think that Hamnet is among the top five best shot films that I’ve seen from 2025. About the Zone of Interest I think that it demonstrates a skilled cinematographer at work, but I don’t think it’s well shot in a way that is worthy of particular mention or commentary.

How does one really work out what, between narrative and visuals, is the principal element making some of the big art house filmmakers’ films stand out, like the films of Tsai Ming Liang, Apichatpong Weerasethakul, Bela Tarr, etc.? Both the story and the visuals are unique. If the narrative is slow and estranging, the temptation is to think of the films’ focus being on the visual, and think of them as being strongly visually unique. But maybe their visuals alone aren’t *that* singular or “great,” but rather are of an ordinary art house auteur pedigree, so to speak. Less narratively bold directors can employ the same cinematographers.
